coredata.dc:title: Nanofabricated versatile electrochemical sensor for Leptospira interrogans detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Society for Biotechnology, Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nagraik, Rupak
Kaushal, Ankur
Gupta, Shagun
Sethi, Sunil
Sharma, Avinash
Kumar, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description: In this report, a LipL32 gene based nanofabricated electrochemical sensor for the detection of Leptospira interrogans has been developed using carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes with gold nanoparticles (c-MWCNTs/nanoAu) electrode and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode was linked to GQDs using 4-aminothiophenol (ATP). The surface modifications on the electrode surface were delineated using Raman spectroscopy and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). 5′-Amino (NH2) labeled single stranded DNA (ssDNA) probe was immobilized on the surface of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode. The electrochemical changes of the developed sensor after hybridization with single stranded complementary DNA of L. interrogans were analyzed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) using 1 mM methylene blue. The sensor showed good linearity with complementary ssDNA concentration ranging from 0.37 to 12 ng/μl. The sensor exhibited high specificity to L. interrogans and showed good reproducibility when stored at 4°C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LipL32 gene
Electrochemical sensor
Graphene quantum dots
Leptospira interrogans
Specificity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Specificity and reproducibility of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DPV Peak A, c-MWCNTs/nanoAu unmodified bare electrode. Peak B, c-MWCNTs/ATP/GQD/nanoAu composite modified bare electrode. Peak C, ssDNA probe immobilized on nano-Au/c-MWCNTs/ATP/GQD electrode. Peak D, dsDNA formed after hybridization of ssDNA of L. interrogans with the immobilized probe at 50 mVs−1 using 1 mM MB in 50 mM PBS, pH 7.
Raman spectra of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode and GQD-modified c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode.
FE-SEM micrograph. (A) c-MWCNTs/nanoAu bare electrode. (B) c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP electrode. (C) c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode. All images were observed at 5 μm scale.

Specificity of LipL32 based sensor using cyclic voltammetry. The peak current (I p) value after treatment with 1.0 ng/μl ss-DNA of S. aureus, E. coli, L. interrogans, L. biflexa and with equivalent concentration of single base mismatch complementary DNA (SBMD) and three base mismatch complementary DNA (TBMD) with respect to probe. Each value is the average of three readings under similar conditions.

DPV (% peak current) of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD/ssDNA composite electrode observed at a regular interval of 30 days for 4 months on storage at 4°C. Each value is the average of three readings under similar conditions.

DPV of (A) c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD/ssDNA composite electrode and (B–G) after hybridization with different concentrations (0.37, 0.75, 1.5, 3, 6, and 12 ng/μl) of ss-DNA of L. interrogans using 1 mM MB in 50 mM PBS, pH 7. The inset shows plot of DPV peak current as a function of target ssDNA concentration.

head.ce:title: Nanofabricated versatile electrochemical sensor for Leptospira interrogans detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Applied Sciences and Biotechnology, Shoolini University, Post Box No. 9, Head Post Office, Solan, Himachal Pradesh 173212, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Applied Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shoolini University, Post Box No.9, Head Post Office, Solan, Himachal Pradesh-173212, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Manesar, Gurugram, Haryana 122413, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity University, Manesar, Gurugram, Haryana-122413, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Postgraduate Institute of Medical Education and Research, Sector-12, Chandigarh 160012, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Postgraduate Institute of Medical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Postgraduate Institute of Medical Education and Research, Sector-12, Chandigarh-160012, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, a LipL32 gene based nanofabricated electrochemical sensor for the detection of Leptospira interrogans has been developed using carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes with gold nanoparticles (c-MWCNTs/nanoAu) electrode and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode was linked to GQDs using 4-aminothiophenol (ATP). The surface modifications on the electrode surface were delineated using Raman spectroscopy and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). 5′-Amino (NH2) labeled single stranded DNA (ssDNA) probe was immobilized on the surface of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode. The electrochemical changes of the developed sensor after hybridization with single stranded complementary DNA of L. interrogans were analyzed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) using 1 mM methylene blue. The sensor showed good linearity with complementary ssDNA concentration ranging from 0.37 to 12 ng/μl. The sensor exhibited high specificity to L. interrogans and showed good reproducibility when stored at 4°C.

ce:sections.ce:para: Leptospira interrogans is a bacterium that causes leptospirosis, an infection which has emerged as a global health problem. A vast number of clinical manifestations are associated with leptospirosis (1). The conventional methods of leptospirosis diagnosis rely on the detection of L. interrogans. The various diagnostic methods available for L. interrogans detection are bacterial culture, polymerase chain reaction, and many serology based methods such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, macroscopic slide agglutination test and hemagglutination assay (2–6). Microscopic agglutination test is the method of choice for leptospirosis diagnosis, and it is based on the detection of anti-L. interrogans antibodies in various samples (7). The conventional methods used for leptospirosis diagnosis suffer from various shortcomings. The disadvantages such as long growth time, complex operation procedure, and cross reactivity are hindering the timely detection of L. interrogans (8). So there is a constant need for new and efficient detection methods for L. interrogans.

In recent times, biosensors have found efficient application in point-of-care molecular diagnostics and pathogen detection. This is attributed to their high sensitivity, specificity low sample volume requirement, and fast response (9,10). Electrochemical biosensors are among the attention seeking biosensors due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, portability, and good readability over a wide concentration range of analyte (11,12). The use of electrodes made up of various materials (C, Au, Pt, Cu) is an important aspect that determines the sensitivity of biosensors in general. Electrodes are modified or fabricated in order to increase the binding surface area for the bioreceptor. The use of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in electrodes is being reported to increase the biochemical response and sensitivity of electrochemical biosensors (13,14). Various types of nanomaterials have been used in bio sensing applications to enhance the sensitivity, limit of detection, and immobilization surface area of the bio-receptor (15,16). The gold nanoparticles have been used extensively by the researchers in biosensor based applications, due to their unique characteristics viz. inert in nature, distinct optical and electronic properties, ample surface area, and ease of modification (17). Another nanomaterial that has received attention in biosensor based studies is the nanoscale modified form of graphene popularly addressed as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs are zero dimensional layers of graphene with distinct physico-chemical properties, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, good electrical conductivity and large surface area (18–20). They can be functionalized with chemicals (linkers) to enhance the connectivity between the bio-receptor and the electrode surface. 4-Aminothiophenol (ATP) belongs to the class of compounds with nanoparticles assembling ability via covalent or electrostatic interactions. It can be used as a linker for better and stable immobilization of the bio-receptor molecules (21).

As LipL32, a 32 kDa outer membrane protein is a conserved protein found in all pathogenic leptospires (22); therefore, in the present study, an electrochemical sensor targeting the LipL32 gene of L. interrogans was fabricated on c-MWCNTs (with gold nanoparticles) electrode. The gold (Au) in the electrode was linked to thiol (-SH) present on ATP. GQDs were linked with amino (-NH2) group of ATP. Further, 5′-NH2 labeled single stranded DNA (ssDNA) capture probe was covalently attached to GQDs via amide bond formation. ssDNA of L. interrogans was allowed to hybridize with the immobilized capture probe. The hybridization event was confirmed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The developed sensor was specific for L. interrogans detection and showed differential binding affinity to single base mismatch and three base mismatch complementary ssDNA. Leptopsira biflexa was used to check the species level specificity.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrodes (3.4 × 1.0 × 0.05 cm) used in the study were of DropSens (Parque Tecnológico de Asturias, Asturias, Spain) and were modified at Shoolini University for the fabrication of the sensor. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimetylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ATP and methylene blue (MB) were of Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA. GQDs were obtained from ACS Material, Padasena, CA, USA. Sodium chloride (NaCl), ethanol (C2H5OH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium di-hydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4), di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4), Tris, ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and other chemicals were from Qualigens, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade. A 20-mer 5′-amino labeled ssDNA probe (5′NH2-TGGCTATCTCCGTTGCACTC-3′) specific to LipL32 gene of L. interrogans, single base mismatch complementary DNA sequence (SBMD, 5′-GAGTTCAACGGAGATAGCCA-3′) and three base mismatch complementary DNA sequence (TBMD, 5′-GAGTTCAACGGAGATATCCC-3′) were obtained from Eurofins, Bengaluru, India. The genomic DNA (G-DNA) used in the present study was isolated at Post Graduate Institute of Medical Education and Research (PGIMER), Chandigarh (23), and was denatured at 95°C (5 min) for further experimental analysis.

In this study, the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode was taken and used for the fabrication of LipL 32 based biosensor. The electrode was washed with autoclaved milli Q water and PBS buffer (pH 7) and then dried at room temperature. The c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode was treated overnight with 6 μl of ATP and washed with autoclaved milli Q water. The electrode was then allowed to dry at room temperature. A mixture of GQDs (12 μl) and 10 mM EDC (6 μl), 10 mM NHS (6 μl) (1:1, v/v in PBS, pH 7) was prepared in a separate Eppendorf and 6 μl of it was added on the electrode surface and kept at room temperature for 4–6 h. The electrode surface was washed with PBS (pH 7) to remove excess reagents and allowed to dry at room temperature. Now, the designed probe (6 μl, 10 μM) amine labeled was placed on the electrode surface, and the setup was left for 6 h in room temperature. The unbound probe was removed by 3–4 washings with TE buffer and allowed to dry at room temperature.

The electrochemical characterization of the developed sensor was elucidated by DPV (FRA2 μAutolab type iii, Metrohm, New Delhi, India) using 50 μl MB (1 mM) in the potential range of 0–0.6 V at 50 mV/s scan rate. The DPV peak current (I p) of the bare c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode was compared with the nanocomposite modified electrode, probe immobilized nanocomposite electrode, and the target DNA containing probe (24).

Raman spectroscopy and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) were done to study the surface modifications of the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode. Raman spectra (Lab RAM HR Evolution, Horiba, Kyoto, Japan) was recorded for the unmodified c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode and c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode modified with GQDs within 1000–3000 cm−1 scan range using the green laser at 532 nm (1800 gr/mm, 3.2% lp, 30 s, 50x lwd).

FE-SEM (Nova Nano SEM-450, FEI Company, Fremont, CA, USA) was done to see the surface modifications that occurred on the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode after the fabrication of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode (25).

For specificity studies, ssDNA of two randomly selected organisms viz. Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli from the Microbiology lab of Shoolini University were taken and allowed to hybridize with the capture probe of the sensor. SBMD and TBMD were also used in the specificity studies to see the I p difference in comparison to the complementary DNA. To check the species level specificity, Leptospira biflexa was used. The cyclic voltammetry I p in each case was compared with the I p recorded with L. interrogans.
The steps involved in the construction and electrochemical characterization of the sensor are shown in Fig. 1 .

The electrochemical study results of the developed sensor are shown in Fig. 2 . The DPV I p of bare c-MWCNTs/nanoAu was 3.7 μA (peak A) whereas the I p of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode was 4.6 μA (peak B). This was due to the increase in the electroactivity of the composite electrode because of GQDs (26). The GQDs have large surface area and a large number of electroactive sites. Due to these characteristics, GQDs interact better with the MB. Hence, there is an increase in the conductivity and I p of the composite electrode as compared to the unmodified c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode. After the modification of the composite electrode with capture probe ssDNA (peak C) and hybridized dsDNA (peak D), further, an increase in I p was observed, showing the interaction of MB with DNA. The I p in the case of dsDNA (6.7 μA) was found higher than that of ssDNA (5.8 μA). MB interacts with the guanine base of DNA molecules. Due to more guanines in dsDNA as compared to ssDNA, the I p recorded was higher in case of dsDNA. This resulted in better interaction of MB with dsDNA as compared to the ssDNA, and more MB molecules can be accumulated on dsDNA that increased the conductivity (27).

Raman spectroscopy was done to characterize the surface modifications of the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode. The comparative Raman spectra before and after the deposition of GQDs on the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode are shown in Fig. 3 . The Raman spectra of unmodified c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode exhibit D, G and 2D band at 1346 cm−1, 1582 cm−1, and 2692 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio (I D /I G ) for the unmodified electrode was calculated to be 0.71. After the deposition of GQDs on the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode, the value of I D /I G increased to 1.01, indicating an increase in the elctroactive sites and –COOH functionalities on the electrode surface (28).

The surface modifications of the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode using FE-SEM are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A exhibits the bare c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode surface. The image shows fibrous mesh like structure of MWCNTs having dot like representation of gold nanoparticles. Fig. 4B shows the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode modified with ATP to form c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP electrode. The addition of ATP resulted in smoothening of the earlier fibrous mesh like image of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode. This image confirms the deposition of ATP on the bare c-MWCNTs/nanoAu electrode resulting in the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP modified electrode. The addition of GQDs to the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP modified electrode further modified the structure of the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP modified electrode, as depicted in Fig. 4C. The changed surface morphology confirmed the deposition of GQDs on the c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP electrode and the development of c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD composite electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reproducibility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The results of specificity and selectivity studies are exhibited in Fig. 5 . The cyclic voltammetry I p corresponding to S. aureus and E. coli ssDNA was observed equivalent to the I p recorded in the case of the capture probe. The I p corresponding to complementary ssDNA of L. interrogans showed an increase as a result of hybridization with the capture probe. The I p recorded in case of SBMD and TBMD was observed lower than that of the complementary ssDNA because of incomplete hybridization with the capture probe. There was no change in I p observed in the case of L. biflexa in comparison to the capture probe and was also found equivalent to the capture probe I p. This was due to the non-availability of complementary DNA and hence, no interaction of MB with the dsDNA (29).

The reproducibility of the sensor (c-MWCNTs/nanoAu/ATP/GQD/ssDNA) was evaluated by measuring the DPV peak current at regular intervals of 30 days over a period of 4 months (Fig. 6 ). The sensor showed good reproducibility for 4 months when stored at 4°C with approximately 13% loss in original DPV current. This shows that the developed sensor is highly reproducible and can be used for the detection of L. interrogans when stored optimally.

DPV analysis was performed to check the sensitivity of the developed sensor. The capture probe immobilized on the composite electrode (shown by peak A) was allowed to hybridize with different concentrations of complementary ssDNA ranging from 0.37 ng/μl to 12 ng/μl (Fig. 7 ). A linear rise in I p (peak B to G) was observed with increasing concentration of target ssDNA (R2 = 0.90). This was due to more interaction of MB with dsDNA and thereby increasing the conductivity (30). After a concentration of 12 ng/μl of target ssDNA, there was no change in I p as all the probe on the electrode surface was utilized, and no more MB molecules can be accommodated, and hence no change in I p was observed. A regenerated working electrode was used in the sensitivity analysis (to reduce experimentation cost), which resulted in some loss of nanomaterial from the electrode surface and a decrease in conductivity.

The developed sensor is a nanocomposite sensor based on the LipL32 gene of L. interrogans. The developed sensor was able to detect complementary ssDNA of L. interrogans with good specificity and reproducibility. The sensor showed good electrochemical response in the form of DPV peak current over a concentration range of target leptospiral ssDNA from 0.37 to 12 ng/μl. The sensor was able to bind differently to SBMD and TBMD ssDNA and also showed change in the DPV peak current in comparison to the target ssDNA. The developed sensor has great potential as a diagnostic tool in detecting leptospirosis, causing L. interrogans. Further, validation of the sensor has to be done on real samples to explore the full potential of the developed sensor as a routine diagnostic tool for leptospirosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. P.K. Khosla, Vice Chancellor, Shoolini University, Solan, H.P., and PGIMER, Chandigarh, for providing the equipment and resources to carry out the present research work. The authors would also like to thank Shoolini University's Scientific Writing Cell for its contribution. The present work was carried out in compliance with the standard ethical norms. The authors do not have any conflict in any regard.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dash, S.K., Sharma, M., Khare, S., Kumar, A.: Carbon-mercaptooctadecane/carboxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite based genosensor for detection of bacterial meningitis, Ind. J. Microbiol., 54, 170-177 (2014).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene electrode modified with electrochemically reduced graphene oxide for label-free DNA detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, B., Pan, G., Avent, N.D., Lowry, R.B., K., Madgett, T.E., Waines, P.L.: Graphene electrode modified with electrochemically reduced graphene oxide for label-free DNA detection, Biosens. Bioelectron., 72, 313-316 (2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free immunosensor based on hyperbranched polyester for specific detection of α-fetoprotein

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Niu, Y., Yang, T., Ma, S., Peng, F., Yi, M., Wan, M., Mao, C., Shen, J.: Label-free immunosensor based on hyperbranched polyester for specific detection of α-fetoprotein, Biosens. Bioelect., 92, 1-7 (2017).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective and sensitive electrochemical CS–MWCNTs/Au-NPs composite DNA biosensor for Staphylococcus aureus gene sequence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, Y., He, X., Ji, J., Jia, M., Wang, Z., Sun, X.: A highly selective and sensitive electrochemical CS-MWCNTs/Au-NPs composite DNA biosensor for Staphylococcus aureus gene sequence detection, Talanta., 141, 300-306 (2015).
